description.task: Implement and compare several hash functions for strings: (1) sum of ASCII values (poor), (2) polynomial rolling hash (good), (3) Java's built-in hashCode (reference). Show distribution quality by counting collisions.
instructions: sumHash: for(char c:s.toCharArray()) sum+=c; return sum%m;
'cat','act','tac' all have ASCII sum 99+97+116=312 → same sum hash = 2; polyHash distinguishes them
Sum hash has many anagram collisions; polynomial hash is much better
Words: 9 words, 10 buckets; sum hash likely has 4-5 collisions; poly hash 2-3
description.hint: sumHash: for(char c:s.toCharArray()) sum+=c; return sum%m; polyHash: for(char c:s.toCharArray()) h=(h*31+c)%m;
description.expectedOutput:
sumHash('cat',10): 2
polyHash('cat',10): 4

Sum hash collisions:  5
Poly hash collisions: 2
Poly is better: true

description.task: Implement Rabin-Karp string search using a rolling hash. Compute the hash of the pattern and use a sliding window hash over the text. When hashes match, verify character-by-character to handle collisions (spurious hits).
instructions: Rabin-Karp is O(n+m) average, O(nm) worst (with many hash collisions)
AABA found at indices [0,9,12]; abc found at [0,3,6]; world not in hello → []
The rolling hash avoids recomputing from scratch at each window position

description.task: Simulate consistent hashing used in distributed systems. Map servers and keys to positions on a virtual ring (hash circle 0..360). Each key is served by the first server clockwise from it. Add/remove a server and see minimal key reassignment.
instructions: Integer k = ring.ceilingKey(h); if(k==null) k=ring.firstKey(); return ring.get(k);
TreeMap.ceilingKey gives the smallest key >= h (first server clockwise)
If no such key (h > all servers), wrap around to ring.firstKey()
When ServerB is removed, only keys that were mapped to ServerB get reassigned — not all keys
This demonstrates consistent hashing's minimal disruption property
description.expectedOutput:
Initial distribution:
  user1 -> ServerA
  user2 -> ServerC
  user3 -> ServerC
  user4 -> ServerB
  user5 -> ServerA

After removing ServerB:
  user1 -> ServerA
  user2 -> ServerC
  user3 -> ServerC
  user4 -> ServerC
  user5 -> ServerA
